Project Python Foundations: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Full-code version Notebook: Sai Advaith Vootukur

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost_of_the_order: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

df.describe().T

count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
order_id 1898.0 1.477496e+06 548.049724 1476547.00 1477021.25 1477495.50 1.477970e+06 1478444.00
customer_id 1898.0 1.711685e+05 113698.139743 1311.00 77787.75 128600.00 2.705250e+05 405334.00
cost_of_the_order 1898.0 1.649885e+01 7.483812 4.47 12.08 14.14 2.229750e+01 35.41
food_preparation_time 1898.0 2.737197e+01 4.632481 20.00 23.00 27.00 3.100000e+01 35.00

Observations: We can see that the¶

  • the minimum time it takes to prepare food is 20 minutes
  • the average time it takes to prepare food is 2.737197e+01
    • To put this in a readable format, we can run the following equation: 2.737197 x 10^1 = 27.37.197
    • Therefore, we can see that the average time it takes to prepare food is around 27.5 minutes
  • the maximum time it takes to prepare food is 35 minutes

In [79]:
# Histograms and Box Plots for numerical variables

numerical_cols = ['cost_of_the_order', 'food_preparation_time', 'delivery_time'] # List of all the columns being used for the histogram and box plots

for col in numerical_cols:
    print(f"Exploring the distribution of '{col}':") # The "f" is necessary here because without it, the title would only say {col} and not the column we want in the title

    # The following block of code will be used to create a histogram
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
    sns.histplot(data=df, x=col, kde=True)
    plt.title(f'Distribution of {col}') # The "f" is necessary here because without it, the title would only say {col} and not the column we want in the title
    plt.show()

    # The following block of code will be used to create a boxplot
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
    sns.boxplot(data=df, x=col)
    plt.title(f'Box Plot of {col}') # The "f" is necessary here because without it, the title would only say {col} and not the column we want in the title
    plt.show()

Exploring the distribution of 'delivery_time':
In [80]:
# Count Plots for categorical variables

categorical_cols = ['cuisine_type', 'rating'] # List of all the columns being used for the bargraphs

for col in categorical_cols:
    print(f"Exploring the distribution of '{col}':") # The "f" is necessary here because without it, the title would only say {col} and not the column we want in the title
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
    sns.countplot(data=df, x=col, order=df[col].value_counts().index) # The "f" is necessary here because without it, the title would only say {col} and not the column we want in the title
    plt.title(f'Distribution of {col}')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Observations: If we were to run line 2 above, we would get a full list of every customer ID and exactly how many orders they have placed. Since we are specifically interested in the top 3, we can use .nlargest(x) after .value_counts() to figure this out.¶

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [95]:
df['rating'].unique()

df['rating'] = df['rating'].replace('Not given', np.nan)
df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df['rating'])

# df.info()

# This line here will allow us to filter for all the restaurants with a rating over 4 and group by the restaurant names for the total ratings.
# We use .count() here since it will count only the valid entires, which we know is only 1162 after converting the column to a numeric column, since it originally came as a object column
greater_than_4_restaurants = df[df['rating']>4].groupby('restaurant_name')['rating'].count().reset_index(name='rating_count')

# Here, we are using the newly created rating_count column and filtering down to only the restaurants that have over 50 ratings
restaurants_over_50 = greater_than_4_restaurants[greater_than_4_restaurants['rating_count']>50]
# print(restaurants_over_50)

# Here, we are merging the new restaurants_over_50 dataframe with df using the "restaurant_name" column, and then immediately creating a new dataframe that creates an average rating column.
# This allows us to figure out the true average rating of all the restaurants
merge_dfrestaurants = pd.merge(df, restaurants_over_50['restaurant_name'], on='restaurant_name', how='inner')
average_rating = merge_dfrestaurants.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating'].mean().reset_index(name='average_rating')
# print(average_rating)

# This final step has us merging all the relevant dataframes, which in this case is the dataframes related to the average values and over 50 rating count dataframes
# By doing this on the shared column "restaurant_name", the new qualified_restaurants dataframe will only include the rows that have values present in both the merging dataframes
qualified_restaurants = pd.merge(restaurants_over_50, average_rating, on='restaurant_name', how='inner')
print(qualified_restaurants)

     restaurant_name  rating_count  average_rating
0        Shake Shack            60        4.278195
1  The Meatball Shop            53        4.511905

Observations: We can see that there are only restaurants qualified to receive the promotional offer: Shake Shack, and The Meatball Shop.¶

Shake Shack has a total rating count of 60 with an average rating of 4.28, while The Meatball Shop has a total rating of 53 with an average rating of 4.51

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [96]:
# Write the code here

# 'cost_of_the_order' > 20 = 25% increase, then add up the total for this
# 'cost_of_the_order' > 5 = 15% increase, then add up the total for this
# add both totals to net_revenue

# This column will allow us to see the total price with the markup
df['total_cost_with_markup'] = np.where(df['cost_of_the_order']>20, df['cost_of_the_order']*1.25, np.where(df['cost_of_the_order']>5, df['cost_of_the_order']*1.15, df['cost_of_the_order']))

# This column will gives us the total revenue the company makes per order
df['net_revenue'] = df['total_cost_with_markup'] - df['cost_of_the_order']

# Total sum of the revenue the company makes
net_revenue = df['net_revenue'].sum()

print('We can see that the net revenue the company generates is $', round(net_revenue, 3), sep='')

df.head()

We can see that the net revenue the company generates is $6166.303
Out[96]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time total_cost_with_markup net_revenue
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend NaN 25 20 38.4375 7.6875
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend NaN 25 23 13.8920 1.8120
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5.0 23 28 14.0645 1.8345
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3.0 25 15 36.5000 7.3000
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4.0 25 24 13.3285 1.7385

Observations: We can see that in total, with the markups the company charges, they are making a total of $6166.30 dollars in net revenue¶

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [97]:
# Write the code here

# Here, we are going to use the columns "Food_preparation_time" and "delivery_time", add the values in the columns together, and create a new column called "total_required_time".
# Then after doing that, we shall find all the orders that take over 60 minutes to deliver, and convert that to a percentage (similar method as in Q9 can be done here)

df['total_required_time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']
orders_over_60 = df[df['total_required_time']>60].shape[0] # We use .shape[0] here since we are trying to know the total amount of rows that have a total delivery time over 60 minutes
print('There are', orders_over_60, 'orders that take over 60 minutes to get delivered after being placed')
print('We know the total amount of orders is', len(df['total_required_time']))
percentage_over_60 = (df[df['total_required_time']>60].shape[0]/len(df['total_required_time']))*100
print('Therefore, the total percentage of orders over 60 minutes is', percentage_over_60, '\nAfter rounding, the percentage is ', round(percentage_over_60,2), '%', sep='')

There are 200 orders that take over 60 minutes to get delivered after being placed
We know the total amount of orders is 1898
Therefore, the total percentage of orders over 60 minutes is10.537407797681771
After rounding, the percentage is 10.54%

Observations: We can see that the total percentage of orders over 60 minutes is around 10.54% of the orders¶

df.groupby('day_of_the_week')['delivery_time'].mean().round(3)

delivery_time
day_of_the_week

Observations: We can see that the mean delivery time varies by around 6 minutes when comparing the weekday delivery times and the weekend delivery times¶

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions:¶

  • On average, the food preparation time ranges from 20 to 35 minutes, and delivery time ranges from 15 to 33
  • A substantial number of orders (736) were not rated. Among the rated orders, the majority have ratings of 4 or 5.
  • Shake Shack and The Meatball Shop are the most popular restaurants in terms of number of orders.
  • American, Japanese, and Italian cuisines are the most popular overall, both during the week and the weekend
  • Based on the commission structure, the company does generate a sizeable revenue on all orders
  • Only two companies meet the requirement for the promotional offer: Shake Shack, and The Meatball Shop. Both uncoincidentally the most popular restaurants as well
  • Approximately 10.5% of all orders take more than 60 minutes, from preparation to end of delivery

Recommendations:¶

  • Due to the lack of people ordering them, I would recommend removing the following cuisine types:

    • Thai
    • Southern
    • French
    • Spanish
    • Vietnamese
    • Korean

    This could help with leveraging the cuisine popularity, and also allow for reducing total costs within the restaurants themselves

  • Implement strategies to encourage more customers to rate their orders. This could include push notifications, in-app reminders, or small incentives. More ratings will provide valuable feedback for restaurants and the platform.

  • Investigate the orders that take longer than 60 minutes for delivery. Identify bottlenecks in the process (food preparation or delivery) for these orders and work with restaurants and delivery personnel to reduce these times. This could involve setting stricter time targets or providing support for efficient order handling.
